turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson has the opinion of the Court today in case 145 in our original docket, Delaware versus Pennsylvania, and 146, Arkansas versus Delaware.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When property is abandoned, a state can sometimes take possession of that property for itself. That process is called escheatment and there are legal rules that govern it. If the abandoned property is physical, the escheatment rules are straightforward. The property escheats to the state in which it is located. But if the abandoned property is intangible, the rules can be more complicated, because many states may try to claim it. This case is about the rules that apply to determine, which state is entitled to escheat certain abandoned intangible property. Money orders provide a good example of intangible property that might implicate escheatment rules. An individual who wants to give money to another person might purchase a money order, and to do so that person would go to a location that sells money orders, perhaps a bank, and give that seller the value of the money order say $100, plus a fee. That $100 will then be available for collection by the intended payee elsewhere and the payee can go to wherever money orders can be cashed to retrieve that money. But if the intended payee never shows up, which sometimes happens, and if the bank or other entity that is holding the $100 prepayment can't refund the purchaser, that money could be deemed abandoned and would then be available for escheatment by a state. This original action between Delaware and an assortment of other states asks us to decide which rules apply to the escheatment of particular financial instruments that MoneyGram, a financial services company incorporated in Delaware, has created. To cut to the chase, we hold that the two MoneyGram products at issue here, which I will call the disputed instruments, are governed by the rules established in the federal statute called the Disposition of Abandoned Money Orders and Traveler's Checks Act, which is known as the FDA. To understand our ruling, it is important to know that there are two different sets of rules that might apply to govern the escheatment of these products. On the one hand, there are rules that have been created by our case law, let's call those common law rules, and on the other, there are rules that were created by Congress in the FDA. Our rules, which were first established in 1965 in a case called Texas versus New Jersey recognized that it was important as a matter of fairness that the abandoned intangible property be distributed among the states in proportion to the commercial activities of their residents rather than to a single state. So we adopted a default rule that funds from abandoned products like money orders should escheat to the state where the purchaser or payee lives, but we also understood that the company that was holding onto the abandoned funds might not keep records that reflect the last known address of the purchaser or payee. So as a backup rule, we said that in that circumstance which we didn't expect to happen very often, the abandoned funds would go to the company's state of incorporation. Well, not long after our ruling, it became clear that the backup rule was operating at the governing standard for some products, because the company holding onto the abandoned funds did not keep records of the relevant addresses as a matter of business practice. So rather than the escheats being fairly distributed to the various states, the abandoned funds were inequitably escheating solely to the state of incorporation of the company holding the funds. Congress passed the FDA to displace our common law rules and fix this problem at least with respect to certain financial instruments. The relevant text of the FDA provides that the abandoned sums related to “money orders, traveler's checks, and other similar written instruments, other than a third-party bank check” should escheat to the state where the product was purchased rather than the state where the purchaser or payee lives or the state of incorporation of the company holding the funds as our rules had established. For the following reasons and as further explained in our written opinion, we hold that the FDA's escheatment rules apply to the disputed instruments, because they qualify as other similar written instruments for FDA purposes based on two key similarities with money orders. First, like money orders, they are prepaid financial instruments that are used to transmit money to an intended payee, and second, the disputed instruments pose the same inequitable escheatment problem that money orders do and that Congress sought to fix in the FDA. That is MoneyGram does not keep records of the addresses of either the individuals who purchase the disputed instruments or the intended recipients. Thus, if our common law rules were to apply, then just as with money orders, these two MoneyGram products would escheat inequitably solely to Delaware if they are abandoned. The FDA is the statutory bandaid that is designed to work in this precise situation. We are also not convinced that the MoneyGram products should be considered third-party bank checks and thereby excluded from the statute. That phrase is not defined in the FDA and does not appear to have an ordinary meaning, and Delaware has not provided a convincing reason why it should apply to the two products at issue here. We are reluctant to read this ambiguous phrase as excluding from the FDA entire swaths of prepaid financial instruments that are otherwise similar to money orders due to the record-keeping deficit, and some of us also note that during the drafting process, Congress referred to the addition of the third-party bank check language as a minor or technical change, which it would not be if we applied it to the instruments such as these. Accordingly, we adopt the special masters recommendations in the first interim report to the extent they are consistent with this opinion, we overrule Delaware's exceptions to that report, and we remand this matter to the special master for further proceedings. The Court's opinion is unanimous, except with respect to part 4B, which Justice Thomas, Justice Alito, Justice Gorsuch, and Justice Barrett do not join.
